name: Split consignment — confirm split
pageTitle: Split consignment - Import and export applications - GOV.UK
heading: Split consignment
bodyCopy: Are you sure you wish to split this consignment?
Splitting this consignment is an option if your goods are highly perishable or you need part of the consignment urgently.

structure.text: Are you sure you wish to split this consignment?
Splitting this consignment is an option if your goods are highly perishable or you need part of the consignment urgently.

govukComponents.usage: The single confirm action — 'Yes, split this consignment'. Submits the wrapping POST form. Carries id="yes-button", data-module="govuk-button" and data-prevent-double-click="true". Corroborated by the page object, which addresses it by accessible role+name (getByRole('button', { name: 'Yes, split this consignment' })) — the rendered accessible name matches the test's expectation exactly.
govukComponents.evidence: trace e1089761b027497068678616998961b638ecf2e0, action 167 (snapshot before; main outerHTML); corroborated ~/git/defra/ipaffs/ipaffs-qa-automation/page-objects/notification/SplitConsignmentPage.ts:6-8

govukComponents.usage: H1 'Split consignment' (govuk-heading-xl, id=page-primary-title) and H2 question 'Are you sure you wish to split this consignment?' (govuk-heading-m).
Explanatory paragraph about perishable/urgent goods (govuk-body govuk-!-margin-bottom-7), and the bare govuk-body paragraph wrapping the decline link.

The decline path — 'No, don't split this consignment' links back to the review page (?source=dashboard). Not a secondary button; a plain link. NOTE: no page object getter and no test exercises this link — the decline path is entirely untested in the QA suite (SplitConsignmentPage.ts exposes only the confirm button; a grep for the link text across page-objects/, tests/ and workflows/ returns zero matches — re-verified this pass).
govukComponents.evidence: trace e1089761b027497068678616998961b638ecf2e0, action 167; absence corroborated ~/git/defra/ipaffs/ipaffs-qa-automation/page-objects/notification/SplitConsignmentPage.ts:3-25 (no decline locator) + grep for "don't split" across page-objects/ tests/ workflows/ = 0 hits

govukComponents.usage: Standard two-thirds content column. Outer div carries id="split-consignment-confirmation-page".

nonStandardPatterns.detail: The 'CHEDPP.GB.2026.1526042 - V1' banner immediately above <main> uses the non-govuk class 'heading-tertiary' on a span wrapping #reference-number and #cved-version-number, inside a div.govuk-!-padding-top-6. It is page chrome shared across the notification journey, not this page's own markup — <main> itself remains 100% govuk-*.

The header Back link is the GOV.UK Back link component (class="govuk-back-link") but carries a non-govuk modifier class 'back-link--next-to-breadcrumbs' and href="#" (JS history-back rather than a real URL). Outside <main>; chrome, not this page's markup.
nonStandardPatterns.evidence: trace e1089761b027497068678616998961b638ecf2e0, action 167 — Back

fields.name: crumb
fields.label: (no visible label — hidden CSRF token)
fields.evidence: trace e1089761b027497068678616998961b638ecf2e0, action 167 — <input name="crumb" type="hidden" value="[REDACTED]">

fields.name: etag
fields.label: (no visible label — hidden optimistic-concurrency token)
fields.evidence: trace e1089761b027497068678616998961b638ecf2e0, action 167 — <input name="etag" type="hidden" value=""0000000000BD2DBD"">
fields.legacyNote: The POST handler DOES validate this field: split_consignment.js:21 runs validator.validate(request.payload) against a Joi schema that spreads ETAG_SCHEMA only (validation/routes/handlers/importer/split_consignment.js:1-13). ETAG_SCHEMA (validation/common_schemas.js:45-52) is Joi.string().min(16).max(18).required(). So at the PAYLOAD level the etag is mandatory (legacy: common_schemas.js:46,48). This does not change the field's UX requiredness — it is a hidden token the page auto-populates, never a user-entered value — so required stays false for the rebuild's form model. The mandatoriness here is a server-side integrity check on a machine-supplied token, and is a policy the rebuild may implement differently (e.g. via HTTP If-Match / ETag headers rather than a form field). No trace exercised a missing/invalid etag, so these messages never rendered. confidence: legacy.

validationMessages.trigger: etag absent from the POST payload (Joi any.required)

Applied to this page via validation/routes/handlers/importer/split_consignment.js:10,21 spreading ETAG_SCHEMA. NOTE: this is a hidden-token integrity error, not user-facing copy — surfaced through common/validationSummary (splitConsignment.html:15) only if the token is missing/tampered. Never rendered in the trace corpus.

Applied via split_consignment.js:10,21. Never rendered in the trace corpus.

validationMessages.trigger: etag payload value is not a string (Joi string.base)
validationMessages.evidence: message built by typeMessage('ETAG', 'string') → `${label} must be a ${type}` (~/git/defra/ipaffs/ipaffs-frontend-notification/service/src/validation/messages/en.js:27-29) with label 'ETAG' (validation/labels/en.js:52); wired at ~/git/defra/ipaffs/ipaffs-frontend-notification/service/src/validation/common_schemas.js:49 ([STRING_TYPE]: typeMessage(errorLabels.ETAG, 'string')). Applied via split_consignment.js:10,21. Never rendered in the trace corpus.

openQuestions: This page collects NO user input. It is a pure confirm/interstitial: one POST form containing only hidden crumb (CSRF) and etag (concurrency) inputs, one submit button, and one decline link. The new app's equivalent needs no form fields — only a confirm action and a cancel link. Independently re-derived: a full-document control inventory at action 167 returns only the cookie-banner button, the two hidden inputs, #yes-button, and the footer cookies form (crumb/returnUrl/fromFooterHeader) — i.e. nothing on this page beyond the two hidden tokens. CORROBORATED by source 2: the page object exposes no input locators at all (SplitConsignmentPage.ts:3-25), and the workflow layer has no fill-this-page function — the only workflow reference is the assertion helper expectSplitTabCommodities (~/git/defra/ipaffs/ipaffs-qa-automation/workflows/notification/ched-pp-workflows.ts:1008). The whole page reduces to a single click (ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:122, 171, 235, 293). Two independent sources agree the page is input-free.

The POST is to the same URL (form has no action attribute) and returns 302 (request #1017 in the trace) redirecting to /notification/vnet/protected/notifications/{ref}/review?source=dashboard. The GET that renders the page is request #1001 (200, 5.9K). Both verified in the trace's request list.

The confirm button sets data-prevent-double-click="true". This looks load-bearing, not cosmetic — a double submit would presumably generate duplicate child CHEDs. The new app should keep an equivalent guard (idempotency on the server, not just client-side). No test exercises a double-click, so the server-side behaviour on repeat submit remains a GAP.

The 'etag' hidden field implies IPAFFS does optimistic concurrency on the notification when splitting (reject the split if the notification changed since the page was rendered). No trace exercised the conflict path and no test asserts on it either — the failure copy/behaviour when the etag is stale remains a GAP after corroboration. Question for a human: what should the new app do when a split is confirmed against a stale view?

No validation messages exist for this page and none could exist — there is nothing to validate. The corpus contains no error state for this URL, and no test in tests/notification/ched-pp/ asserts any error message against this page. `gap` is the honest answer only in the trivial sense: there is no rule to state.

<main> is 100% govuk-* — zero non-standard classes inside the main region. This page can be rebuilt inside the govuk-frontend toolbox with no custom CSS or bespoke widgets. Two non-standard classes DO appear in the surrounding chrome (heading-tertiary on the reference banner; back-link--next-to-breadcrumbs on the Back link) — both recorded under nonStandardPatterns, both outside this page's own markup. Worth noting for the rebuild: GDS's own pattern for a yes/no confirmation would more usually be a Button + secondary link exactly as done here, so this page is already idiomatic.

PRECONDITION (inferred) — REFINED THIS PASS by reading all four split tests' entry states in full. Source 2 states the entry route explicitly: the split is reached only from the 'Split consignment' link on the review page (ReviewNotificationPage.ts:22-24). There are FOUR split tests in ched-pp-notification.spec.ts; only THREE carry the @chedpp-split tag (tag lines 143, 211, 266; the enclosing test( calls open at 141, 209, 264) — the fourth (test( at 81, title at 82, tag at 83) is tagged @dynamics. The decision state at the moment the split link is clicked, in each: (a) @dynamics test — first=Compliant, second=Non compliant; notification status 'Partially rejected' asserted on BOTH the decision CHED overview and the notification dashboard before the link is used (ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:102, 105, 112, 117). (b) first=Compliant, second=Hold, third=Hold → split still offered, producing only a V child (ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:162-177). (c) first=Compliant, second=Hold (ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:228-237). (d) first=Hold, second=Compliant — the Compliant one is the SECOND commodity (ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:281-288, assertion at 299 confirms the V child holds adenariaFloribunda, the second commodity). So the gate is 'at least one commodity Compliant AND at least one not-yet-Valid', NOT specifically 'one non-compliant', and case (d) newly establishes that COMMODITY ORDER IS IRRELEVANT — the Compliant commodity need not be the first. 'Partially rejected' is therefore sufficient but not necessary to reach the split. confidence: inferred.

TEST-CODE DEFECT FOUND THIS PASS (a finding for the QA repo, not a requirement). In the third @chedpp-split test the comment at ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:286 reads '// Part 3: B2B inspector records first=Compliant' but the code on the very next line (:288) records the decision against the SECOND commodity: recordDecisionForChedPPNotification(inspectorPages, referenceNumber, 'Compliant', 'second'). The code — not the comment — is authoritative: the assertion at :299 expects the Valid tab to contain adenariaFloribunda, which is the second commodity (:274). The comment at :279 ('Part 2: … records first=Hold, second=Hold') is correct. So the test is self-consistent and passing; only the :286 comment is wrong. This matters because a reader trusting the comment would wrongly conclude the split always requires the FIRST commodity to be Compliant — the opposite of what this test actually proves (see the precondition note above). Worth a one-line fix in the QA repo.

CHILD-CHED REFERENCE FORMAT — GAP NOW CLOSED by source 2. The trace never rendered the suffixes; the tests assert them as literal string templates: valid children are `${referenceNumber}V` and rejected children are `${referenceNumber}R`, surfaced on the review page as the copy 'Valid commodities split into - {ref}V' and 'Rejected commodities split into - {ref}R' (ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:174-176, 187-190, 237-241, 252-253; locator ReviewNotificationPage.ts:26-28, a getByText(/commodities split into -/i) regex). NOTE: the @dynamics test at :130 asserts the same copy WITHOUT the V/R suffix (toContainText with a bare `${referenceNumber}`) — that is a weaker substring assertion, not a contradiction, since toContainText matches a prefix. confidence: inferred (asserted by tests, not observed rendered in our corpus).

OUTCOME STATUS — GAP NOW CLOSED by source 2. After a successful split the ORIGINAL CHED's status becomes 'Split consignment' on the decision CHED overview (ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:205, 260, 316 — asserted in three separate tests, so it is a deliberate rule, not incidental). The parent notification is not deleted or replaced; it persists as a split parent. confidence: inferred.

SPLIT IS INCREMENTAL, NOT ONE-SHOT — a behaviour the traces never exercised and the single most important thing the tests add. In ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:168-201 the split is confirmed ONCE (line 171) while two commodities are still on Hold, producing only a V child. Decisions recorded AFTER the split (Hold → Non compliant at line 181; Hold → Compliant at line 196) then flow into the existing children — the R child appears at line 189 without the split being re-confirmed, and the third commodity joins the V tab at line 200. So confirming the split establishes a standing arrangement that later decisions populate. The new app must not model split as a single atomic partition computed at confirm time. confidence: inferred.

DECISIONS ARE RE-RECORDABLE / OVERWRITABLE (inferred, noted this pass). The tests move a commodity Hold → Non compliant (:181) and Hold → Compliant (:196, :288) by simply re-running recordDecisionForChedPPNotification against the same commodity — no separate 'amend decision' route. Combined with the incremental-split rule above, this means the parent's partition is a live projection of current decisions, not a snapshot. The new app needs to answer: what happens to an already-issued V child if a commodity inside it is later re-decided as Non compliant? No test covers that reversal — it is a GAP and a question for a human.

The SplitConsignmentPage page object bundles tabValid / tabRejected / panelValid / panelRejected (SplitConsignmentPage.ts:10-24), but these controls do NOT live on this page — they render on the REVIEW page after the 302. This is a page-object grouping convenience, not evidence of controls on this URL. The trace agrees (tabs appear at actions 169-170, post-redirect). Confirmed again this pass by the workflow helper: expectSplitTabCommodities' own docstring says the panels are 'on the notification dashboard review page after a CHED-PP split' (~/git/defra/ipaffs/ipaffs-qa-automation/workflows/notification/ched-pp-workflows.ts:1000-1007), and the tests re-navigate to the review page via openForChed BEFORE asserting on the tabs (:172, :236, :294). Recorded so a reader of the page object is not misled into putting tabs on the confirm page.

TAB PANELS RENDER BOTH ARMS IN THE DOM (inferred, noted this pass). expectSplitTabCommodities asserts with toContainText and carries an explicit comment that this 'works even when the panel is the inactive tab (GOV.UK Frontend tabs hide non-active panels with display:none)' (~/git/defra/ipaffs/ipaffs-qa-automation/workflows/notification/ched-pp-workflows.ts:1011-1012 — the comment sits inside the for-loop above the toContainText assertion at :1013; an earlier pass of this spec miscited it as :1016-1018, which is past the file's end at 1015 lines). So the review page server-renders both the Valid and Rejected panels and lets the govuk Tabs component hide one client-side, rather than fetching a panel per tab. Relevant to the rebuild only if the new app reuses the tab pattern on the post-split review page — it is not a property of THIS page.

A reference/version banner 'CHEDPP.GB.2026.1526042 - V1' renders immediately ABOVE
, outside the main region, as
… - V1
. It is not a govuk-caption-* and not part of this page's own markup, so it is recorded as context (and under nonStandardPatterns for the heading-tertiary class) rather than as the page caption. If the new app wants a section caption above the H1, it would be a new design decision.

The header 'Back' link IS the GOV.UK Back link component (class="govuk-back-link") but is wired to href="#" (JS history-back) rather than a real URL, and carries a non-govuk 'back-link--next-to-breadcrumbs' modifier. It is nav chrome outside <main>. No backLink is recorded for this page; the decline link serves the cancel path.

Action 171 (asserting on locator('#valid')) is the trace's single failure — confirmed via `trace actions --errors-only`, which reports exactly one failing action. It is an assertion on the REVIEW page, not this page, so it does not undermine this page's evidence. No trace/test disagreement was found for any confirmed claim on this page: everything source 2 says about the confirm button matches what rendered. Re-verified by adversarial pass v33: SplitConsignmentPage.ts:6-8 and :10-24, ReviewNotificationPage.ts:22-24 and :26-28, ched-pp-workflows.ts:1000-1007 and :1008, and every ched-pp-notification.spec.ts line reference (81/83, 102, 105, 112, 117, 122, 130, 143, 162-166, 171, 174-176, 181, 187-190, 196, 200, 205, 211, 266, 282-288, 299, 316) each resolve to the claimed content. CORRECTION: a prior pass asserted that EVERY citation had been re-read and resolved; that assertion was false — the display:none comment was cited at ched-pp-workflows.ts:1016-1018, which is past the end of a 1015-line file. It is now corrected to :1011-1012. Treat blanket 'all citations verified' statements in a spec with suspicion; they are cheap to write and were, here, wrong.

ACCESSIBILITY GAP: tests/accessibility/ched-pp-accessibility-tests.spec.ts — which the brief describes as naming every page in journey order — contains NO reference to the split consignment page (case-insensitive grep for 'split' returns 0 lines; re-verified this pass). This page is therefore not covered by the a11y sweep. Question for a human: is that a deliberate exclusion (inspector-only, B2B page) or an oversight? Relevant because the new app will want this page in its a11y suite.

Journey position -1 is retained: this page sits OFF the notifier's create journey. It is a B2B INSPECTOR page — every test reaches it via pagesWithAuth(browser, AUTH_FILES.b2bInspector) (ched-pp-notification.spec.ts:99-100, 160-161, 226-227, 279-280), never as the b2cNotifier who created the notification. If the new CHED-PP app is notifier-facing only, this page may be out of scope entirely — worth confirming with a human before it is rebuilt.

HEADING STRUCTURE (rebuild note, independently observed at action 167): the H1 is the generic 'Split consignment' while the actual decision — 'Are you sure you wish to split this consignment?' — is demoted to an H2 (govuk-heading-m) inside the form, and the <title> follows the H1 not the question. This is a govuk component used in a non-standard way rather than a non-govuk class, so it does not show up in the class inventory. GDS guidance for a single-question confirmation page is to make the question itself the H1, so that title, H1 and question agree; here a screen-reader user hears 'Split consignment' as the page identity and must read on to reach the decision. Noted as a deliberate improvement for the rebuild rather than a faithful port — it slightly qualifies the 'already idiomatic' verdict above, which holds for the Button + decline-link pattern but not for the heading hierarchy. confidence: confirmed (markup); the recommendation itself is a design judgement for a human.

Evidence pointer was correct — action 167 is on this page. Independently verified: page title 'Split consignment - Import and export applications - GOV.UK', H1 'Split consignment', and the click at action 167 resolves to <button id="yes-button"> before navigating to the review page. All body copy in this spec was diffed character-for-character against main.innerText at action 167 and matches exactly.

LEGACY TEMPLATE READ (this pass) — the IPAFFS source template is ~/git/defra/ipaffs/ipaffs-frontend-notification/service/src/views/importer/splitConsignment.html (route wired at handlers/importer/split_consignment.js, VIEW_TEMPLATE 'importer/splitConsignment'). It confirms the rendered trace character-for-character with ZERO discrepancies: H1 'Split consignment' via the common/pageTitle partial (splitConsignment.html:16); H2 'Are you sure you wish to split this consignment?' (govuk-heading-m, :22-24); the perishable/urgent paragraph (govuk-body govuk-!-margin-bottom-7, :26-28) verbatim; the button 'Yes, split this consignment' (govuk-button govuk-!-margin-bottom-4, id=yes-button, data-prevent-double-click=true, :30-32); the decline link 'No, don't split this consignment' (govuk-link, :34-37). No conditional {{#if isChedpp}} branch and no hidden CHED-PP-only control exists on this template — the page is identical for all notification types. So there are NO missed fields to add: the template's <form> contains exactly the two hidden inputs (crumb :19, etag :20) already in the spec, one button, one link. confidence: legacy (template read); corroborates the confirmed trace.

DECLINE LINK HREF — legacy fills the GAP the trace left as a raw URL. The decline link's href is the template variable {{cancelUrl}} (splitConsignment.html:35), and the handler sets cancelUrl = REVIEW_PAGE(referenceNumber) + '?source=dashboard' (split_consignment.js:40,44-46) — the same review URL the successful POST 302-redirects to (getReviewPageUrl, :32). So confirm and decline both land on the review page; decline is a plain GET navigation, confirm is the POST that triggers splitNotification. This matches the trace's observed 302 target exactly. confidence: legacy.

STALE-ETAG behaviour — legacy REFINES (does not fully close) the GAP. The frontend validator only checks the etag's PRESENCE and 16–18-char length synchronously (common_schemas.js:45-52); it does not detect staleness. The actual optimistic-concurrency decision is delegated to the backend: split_consignment.js:29-30 calls notificationService.splitNotification(..., request.payload.etag), passing the token to the notification microservice. So 'what happens when a split is confirmed against a stale view' is decided server-side in the microservice, not in this page's validator — the frontend has no stale-etag error copy of its own (the three etag messages above are missing/format/type only). The human question stands: the microservice's conflict response/copy is outside this frontend repo and was not in the corpus. confidence: legacy (frontend scope); backend conflict copy remains a GAP.

VALIDATION SUMMARY partial — the template includes {{> common/validationSummary}} at splitConsignment.html:15, the standard GOV.UK error-summary region. It renders only when the handler populates viewContext.errors (split_consignment.js:24-26, on Joi failure). Since the sole validated payload field is the hidden etag, the ONLY errors this summary can ever show are the three etag integrity messages above — never a user-input error, because the page has no user input. confidence: legacy.
